title: Untimely Deaths Continue Under the “Amma” Regime!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil reading copy below corrects clear OCR character, spacing, and word-boundary errors encountered during translation; the canonical source document remains unchanged. Newspaper reports, allegations, and quoted judgments remain explicitly attributed. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: An eleven-storey building at Moulivakkam near Porur in Chennai, permitted by the Jayalalithaa government and still under construction, collapsed and claimed 61 priceless lives. Jayalalithaa has not come forward to take appropriate action against the minister or others who wrongly and irregularly granted permission for its construction. Instead, she has announced a nominal, eyewash inquiry commission to deceive others. Condemning this and insisting that the Tamil Nadu government arrange a CBI inquiry into what happened, the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam has announced that the Chennai and Kanchipuram district units will conduct a massive march towards Raj Bhavan at around 10 a.m. on 12 July.
Though the march has been announced on behalf of the Chennai and Kanchipuram district units, I trust that Kazhagam comrades from neighbouring districts will also take part.

When this eleven-storey building collapsed on 28 June, 61 people were killed and 27 were receiving hospital treatment. After visiting the site, Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa initially announced financial assistance of only two lakh rupees for each bereaved family. But after Andhra Pradesh Chief Minister Chandrababu Naidu visited and announced five lakh rupees for each family from his State, Jayalalithaa announced a further five lakh rupees from the Tamil Nadu government for every bereaved family.

Under the title "Scriptures that Feed on Corpses," journalist Samas wrote a moving full-page article on the Moulivakkam tragedy in *The Hindu Tamil*. The illustrated article opened with these words: "More than a week has passed. To this day, not even one person has accepted responsibility and resigned, even as a token conventional gesture. Not one person has been removed from office or service. Not one person connected with the government administration has been arrested on corruption charges."
How could they resign? Whom could they dismiss? They must be wondering what would happen if action were taken against someone and he revealed what percentage he had passed on to those above him.

Samas continues: "Even we, watching from a distance, cannot bear it. How are all of them able to remain at peace? Is there such a thing as a conscience, or is there not? A week has passed since it happened, and my hand recoils from calling it an accident. Was it an accident? Or an eruption of corruption? What happened was murder. Murder committed by the corrupt who have festered at every level of this system. Every person's conscience knows this. R. Vaithilingam, Tamil Nadu Housing Minister and Chairman of the Chennai Metropolitan Development Authority, the CMDA, which must bear responsibility for this incident, did not even visit the site."

Samas belongs neither to the DMK nor to any other party. When an independent journalist writes with such anguish in a neutral newspaper, all we ask is whether this does not warrant a CBI inquiry. Because this government has given no answer, we are marching towards Raj Bhavan on the 12th.

M. G. Devasahayam, a retired IAS officer and member of the committee appointed by the Madras High Court to monitor the CMDA, has said: "The fundamental causes of the building collapse were the absence of proper CMDA inspection and the fact that the CMDA has become corruption in its total form. But the Chief Minister's response shows that she is trying to protect the department's officials. If someone is constructing multiple storeys contrary to planning approval, whose responsibility is it to stop that? Is it not the CMDA's? If a building is being constructed contrary to approval, should it not stop the work? Why did the officials fail to do so? Had they intervened, could they not have prevented this unbearable tragedy? Did they perform their duty? They did not."

It is alleged that numerous irregularities occurred in granting permission for the collapsed building. I discussed them at length in my letter dated 4 July 2014. Newspapers have also reported that the departmental minister personally intervened to secure approval. Yet, for reasons of her own, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa appears intent on protecting that minister. Without taking any action against him, she appointed Justice Raghupathi as a one-person inquiry commission and gave the commission no deadline. The judge already holds several responsibilities. It is also said that, even while practising as a lawyer, he worked for the AIADMK.

Before announcing the commission, the Chief Minister had already declared in an interview that there had been no irregularity in granting approval. This can only be regarded as an indirect instruction to the judge heading the inquiry.

According to a report in *Nakkheeran* quoting Moulivakkam Panchayat President Sadasivam, an AIADMK minister performed the ceremonial launch and began construction of the building in July 2013. Because there was a severe sand shortage in 2013, crusher dust was reportedly used. It is also said that even the required quantity of hard-stone aggregate was not used in the pillars. The magazine reported that when the Chief Minister visited the collapsed building, that minister accompanied her. There is also an allegation that seawater was used in construction for one week.

Whenever a government headed by Jayalalithaa has come to power in Tamil Nadu, untimely deaths of this kind have continued to occur. For example, in February 1992, when the Chief Minister and her companion whom she called her sister entered the Mahamaham tank at Kumbakonam to bathe, more than a hundred people in the crowd watching them lost their lives. Even the Sankaracharya described that spectacle at the time as, "She pours for her, and the other pours for her."

In August 2001, a fire at Erwadi killed 28 people. In January 2004, a fire at a wedding in Srirangam burned 54 people to death. In August of the same year, 94 children were burned to death in a school fire at Kumbakonam. Again, in 2005, a stampede during flood-relief distribution at MGR Nagar killed 42 people, 23 women and 19 men. That too occurred under AIADMK rule. Just one month earlier, six women had died in a crowd crush among people who had come to receive flood-relief materials in North Chennai.

Following the MGR Nagar tragedy, an inquiry commission was constituted under retired Justice Raman. Dhanasekaran, who served as the local Kazhagam secretary, was arrested and imprisoned. Even after the Chennai Principal Sessions Court granted him bail on 26 December 2005, the AIADMK government secured an interim stay against his release and invoked the Goondas Act so that he could not come out of prison even on bail. When the Madras High Court held that the bail granted to Dhanasekaran was proper, the government appealed to the Supreme Court.

In their judgment, Supreme Court Justices K. G. Balakrishnan and Navlekar firmly stated: "The irresponsible officials of your government, the Tamil Nadu government, caused this tragedy. Your government bears full responsibility. Do not try to escape by placing the blame on someone else and saying that somebody spread a rumour. A rumour is something whose origin is unknown. But your government says that Dhanasekaran and a few others spread it. The government is responsible. It is the duty of the police to make security arrangements wherever the public gather. Claiming that a rumour caused pushing and a stampede in which 42 people died is irresponsible. Providing security is entirely the responsibility of officials. This is not the first such disaster in Tamil Nadu. Such incidents occur repeatedly. You cannot escape by blaming Dhanasekaran. We dismiss the Tamil Nadu government's appeal."

The party that received judgments of this kind is once again governing Tamil Nadu. It is now following the same old pattern in the Moulivakkam collapse, appointing an inquiry commission under a judge of its choosing and attempting to escape.

One day after announcing the judicial inquiry, and even before that inquiry began, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa announced that a Special Investigation Team under the supervision of the Chennai Police Commissioner would investigate the collapse and that experts from various fields would provide technical advice. If people under the AIADMK government's control conduct the inquiry, they certainly will not expose the government's mistakes. Such inquiries will merely deceive the public. That is why we have demanded a CBI inquiry, a demand supported by many others.

And what is the position of those who bought homes in the Moulivakkam building? Each obtained a bank loan or borrowed elsewhere, paid the money and purchased a flat. What is their fate now? Will their money be returned? What answer does the government have for them? It has even become uncertain whether workers will now be willing to undertake construction work.

Only a thorough inquiry and report can resolve these many questions. That is why the Kazhagam demanded a CBI inquiry. With the AIADMK government giving no response, we are conducting a march to appeal to the Governor. The responsibility for making that march a success rests with you.

Those in neighbouring districts must not think that this is merely a Chennai and Kanchipuram district march. Sixty-one people, worn down by labouring all day in the hope that they could at least work as construction workers and support their families, have been sacrificed. Their family members cry out in anguish: "Where is my wife? Where is my husband? Where is my son?" It is with that suffering in mind that the DMK has come forward to conduct this march.

I have no confidence that, before then, the AIADMK government will announce a CBI inquiry, bring the truth before the world and take action against those responsible for the corruption. Therefore, with the conviction that joining this march is our duty, I call upon every Kazhagam Udanpirappu, in sorrow, to participate in the march on the 12th.
With affection,
M. K.
8 July 2014
